header: WORKUPa
content:
PRMS-1
a Principles of Imaging.
b RMS Site-Specific Considerations (PRMS-2).
c Significant loss of form and function is subjective and decided as part of the multidisciplinary discussion. As a general rule, orbital exenteration, major excisions of the 
face, extremity amputation, rendering an extremity useless, major organ resection (ie, bladder/prostate resection, pneumonectomy), pelvic exenteration, or resection 
requiring permanent stool/urinary diversion should all be avoided. Craniofacial resection for anterior skull-base tumors of the nasal areas, paranasal sinuses, temporal 
fossa, and other such sites should be reserved for surgical teams who demonstrate expert performance in these areas.
d Principles of Pathologic Assessment.
e Germline testing is recommended in selected patients based upon histology and family history.
f PRE should be considered if there is: 1) gross residual tumor; 2) microscopically involved margins; or 3) uncertainty as to margins or residual disease. PRE is 
advisable and should be applied wherever feasible, unless the resulting disability is considered unacceptable (ie, resulting in loss of function or an unacceptable 
cosmetic result). 
g Recommended for patients with parameningeal RMS.
h Recommended for: 1) patients with FOXO1 gene fusion-negative RMS with N1 nodal disease and/or metastatic disease; and 2) all patients with FOXO1 gene fusion-
positive RMS.
i After induction chemotherapy, the primary site should be re-evaluated radiographically and local control should be discussed with a multidisciplinary approach including 
oncology, surgery, and radiation oncology. The discussion should weigh the morbidity of a delayed primary excision (DPE) in which an R0 resection without significant 
loss of form and function can be performed against the morbidity of a higher dose of local radiation therapy (RT).

entire compartment may not be required, depending on the size and invasiveness of the specific tumor.

s Initial radical surgery is not indicated for some genitourinary RMS sites (ie, vulva, vagina). Conservative surgical intervention for vaginal RMS, with primary 
chemotherapy and RT [external beam RT (EBRT) or brachytherapy] for Group II or III disease, results in excellent 5-year survival rates. See Principles of Surgery.
t Initial radical hysterectomy may be considered for patients who do not desire future fertility after shared decision-making with their treating team. For patients who 
desire future fertility, recommend biopsy followed by local control with attempts at preserving fertility. Lautz TB, et al. Pediatr Blood Cancer 2023;70:e28601. See 
Principles of Surgery and Principles of Survivorship.
u Walterhouse DO, et al. Pediatr Blood Cancer 2011;57:76-83; Minard-Colin V, et al. Pediatr Blood Cancer 2018;65:e27096.

inguinal recurrence may follow. If biopsy is felt to be necessary prior to orchiectomy, then atraumatic high spermatic cord control should be performed, and the

Group IIa.

a Perform imaging before biopsy to avoid interpretation difficulties related to inflammatory changes.
b Treatment should be performed as close as possible to initial imaging staging, and imaging may be repeated if there is an interval of longer than 4–8 weeks. 
c Children's Oncology Group (COG) protocols recommend regional draining node sampling in tumors of the trunk and extremities. In addition, RPLN sampling is 
recommended for paratesticular tumors in patients >10 years. See Principles of Lymph Node Disease Assessment.
d In case of low-risk tumor(s) in localized disease, follow-up imaging may be prescribed less intensively with intervals of 6–12 months or as clinically indicated.
e Patients with negative chest CT or indeterminate pulmonary nodules at staging (one to four nodules <5 mm or one nodule measuring 5–10 mm) may be followed with 
chest x-ray.
f CT with contrast if there is any clinical concern for hilar adenopathy; otherwise noncontrast CT may be sufficient if radiologist determines CT of diagnostic quality.
g Involved sites should be followed with the same imaging modality used at baseline with intervals of 3–6 months, as a shared decision between physician, patient, and 
caregiver.
h Primary site MRI imaging may be repeated every 4–6 months.
i Chest x-ray may be obtained every 4–6 months; if any suspicious finding is noted, further assessment with chest CT is recommended.
j Initial staging CT of primary site is done only if it is a presurgical planning request (CT angiography [CTA]) or to assess cortical bone disease extent.

header: PRINCIPLES OF IMAGING1-6
content:
General Principles:
• Imaging using x-ray, CT, FDG-PET/CT, FDG-PET/MRI, or lymphoscintigraphy should be used. Follow the as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA) 
and Image Gently Campaign principles.
• Motion artifact can often be mitigated by using advanced scanning and reconstruction techniques (eg, motion correction software). After considering 
these motion mitigation techniques, anesthesia should be used only if there is a possibility of obtaining a non-diagnostic scan. In addition, anesthesia 
time should be limited to the minimum reasonable time.
• Gadolinium and iodine-based contrast agents should be used carefully when kidney function is decreased or there is a possibility of pregnancy.
MRI:
• Benefits:
High soft tissue contrast providing details regarding tumor location and its boundaries with surrounding anatomic structures to be used in surgical or 
radiation planning and for prognostication.
No ionizing radiation.
• Limitations: 
Relatively limited availability and expensive. Perform FDG-PET/MRI if available.
Long acquisition time and sensitive to patient motion. 
May be contraindicated if certain metallic devices/foreign bodies are present.
Complications related to gadolinium (allergy, nephrogenic systemic fibrosis, and unknown effects of gadolinium deposition in brain and bones).
MRI may be limited in assessing areas near metal (eg, metal clips, prosthesis).
• Acquisition: 
Field of view should include both primary tumor and lymphatic draining station.
Both pre- and post-contrast sequences are optimal.
 ◊Pre-contrast sequences: T1 fat saturation (FS), T1 turbo spin echo (TSE), T2 FS, and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) in the axial plane; short tau 
inversion recovery (STIR) and T1 TSE in the coronal plane; and T2 FS in the sagittal plane.
 ◊Post-contrast: T1 FS in at least 2 planes.
Similar scanner, coils, and protocol should be used across multiple examinations in the same patients when possible.
Advanced techniques may be used to reduce scan time (eg, parallel imaging, radial k-space sampling).
Radiologist may modify imaging protocol according to specific clinical needs.
Ultrasound:
• Operator-dependent.
• Can complement but should not replace MRI. 
• Is often the first imaging modality used in the assessment of soft tissue lesions due to the lack of ionizing radiation and easy accessibility. 
• May provide additional information (eg, mass characterization cystic/solid/vascular) for small or superficial lesions or a paratesticular location.
References

Discussion
Chest X-Ray:
• Widely available, low ionizing radiation.
• Should be obtained in 2 views (posteroanterior and lateral).
• Sensitivity in detection of lung nodules is limited.
• Suspicious findings should be investigated with CT.
CT:
• Chest CT is ideal for assessment of pulmonary metastasis.
• Contrast-enhanced CT of the primary site may be warranted when MRI is contraindicated or in addition to MRI for the assessment of cortical bone 
involvement. 
• CT angiography (CTA) may be necessary for surgical planning to evaluate specific vascular landmarks. 
• Images should be reconstructed with a slice thickness of <5 mm (optimally 1 mm) and lung filter; maximum intensity projection (MIP) may help detect 
solid pulmonary nodules; coronal and sagittal reconstructions should also be carefully inspected.
18-F FDG (Fluorodeoxyglucose) FDG-PET with CT or MRI for Attenuation Correction and Anatomic Localization:
• Scan should be whole body acquisition, using pediatric FDG dose adjustment per institution protocol.
• Advanced techniques may be used to reduce scan time (eg, use of long axial field of view scanners when available).
• FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI can be used for assessing primary tumor (may guide biopsy by visualization of areas with prominent metabolic activity), 
in-transit metastasis, and local and distal lymph nodes and metastases. 
• FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI may visualize bone lesions; however, when bone marrow evaluation is indicated it should not replace bone marrow 
aspirates and biopsies at staging.
• Given the high prevalence of high brown fat in the pediatric population, consider mitigation plans including warm blanket or pharmacologic means (eg, 
propranolol, diazepam).
Lymphoscintigraphy:
• Noninvasive scintigraphic assessment can be obtained with technetium-99m (Tc-99m) tilmanocept or Tc-99m filtered sulfur colloid.
• Single-photon emission CT (SPECT)/CT to improve anatomic localization may be performed.
• Addition of intraoperative methylene blue improves assessment of sentinel lymph nodes.

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGIC ASSESSMENT
content:
Pathologic Assessment
• It is essential to ensure that pathology workflows are structured to integrate histologic diagnosis, prognostic classification, and molecular profiling as 
needed for treatment purposes.
• Specimens obtained for tissue diagnosis must serve multiple objectives:
Establish the diagnosis.
Recognize features that determine prognosis and/or influence treatment.
Detect any features of underlying genetic syndromes.
Store materials for future patient care needs, tumor banking, research, or clinical trial enrollment.
• Tissue sample consideration for primary diagnosis (tumor or metastasis):1-3
Surgical resection if clinically indicated.
Incisional biopsy: >1 cm3.
Tissue cores: at least 10 cores, ideally 20–30 mm in length obtained using a 16-gauge needle.
• A coordinated diagnostic sampling effort including pathologists, oncologists, surgeons, and radiologists is recommended.2-3
• The pathologic assessment should include evaluation of the following features, all of which should be specifically addressed in the pathology report:
Histologic subtype 
Presence or absence of anaplasia 
Therapy effect (if applicable)
Status of margins of excision
Status of lymph nodes (if available for examination) (incisional biopsy preferred) (See Principles of Lymph Node Disease Assessment)
Distant metastasis (if available for examination)
Stage (see ST-1)
Results of ancillary studies
• Histologic classification: 
The International Classification of Rhabdomyosarcoma categorizes childhood RMS into histologic types that have prognostic relevance. Nonetheless, 
research indicates that the FOXO1 fusion status is a stronger predictor of negative outcomes in children with RMS, making histologic classification 
less crucial for prognosis and risk assessment. Despite this, a consistent and accurate classification of histologic subtype remains significant for its 
broad applications.4-6 
The current World Health Organization (WHO) classification for soft tissue and bone tumors recognizes 4 distinct subtypes of RMS:7
 ◊Embryonal
 ◊Alveolar
 ◊Spindle cell/sclerosing
 ◊Pleomorphic (more common in adults; most pleomorphic RMS in children are anaplastic embryonal RMS [ERMS]).

header: PRINCIPLES OF ANCILLARY TECHNIQUES USEFUL IN THE DIAGNOSIS OF RMS
content:
• The diagnosis of RMS requires a combination of morphologic features combined with ancillary testing:
Hematoxylin and eosin stain. 
Immunohistochemistry (IHC) stains: Desmin, myogenin, MYOD1.
 ◊Staining pattern for myogenin can help in subtyping RMS (eg, diffuse staining is typical for alveolar subtype).
 ◊Additional IHC stains may also be useful in confirming the subtype, including AP-2 beta, HMGA2, P-cadherin, and EGFR.1,2
• Molecular Genetic Testing Considerations:
The diagnosis of RMS has undergone significant evolution over the past decade. We now recognize that morphology is shaped by underlying 
molecular alterations.
 ◊Recurring genetic or molecular alterations are listed in Table 1.
Methods/technique:a,3
 ◊Conventional karyotyping.  
 ◊Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH). 
 ◊Reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR). 
 ◊Next-generation sequencing (NGS). 
The choice of the most suitable technique depends on the specific tumor characteristics and clinical requirements. NGS could be advantageous, 
but the timing and suitability of NGS should be assessed on a case-by-case basis. NGS results can help determine if a patient is eligible for clinical 
trials, identify actionable mutations that were not previously targeted, select candidates who might benefit from immunotherapy, and identify inherited 
germline variants associated with developing RMS. Each molecular testing method has its limitations and potential for false-negative results. If 
negative results occur when a molecular aberration was clinically anticipated, it is advisable to consult with the testing laboratory, as retesting using a 
different method may be warranted.
 ◊FOXO1 status should be assessed molecularly. FISH for FOXO1 is sufficient.
a Molecular genetic analysis involves highly complex test methods. None of the methods are absolutely sensitive or provide results that are absolutely specific; test 
results must always be interpreted in the context of the clinical and pathologic features of the case. Testing should therefore be carried out by a pathologist with 
expertise in sarcoma diagnosis and molecular diagnostic techniques.

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY
content:
General Principles
• Local control with either primary tumor excision or RT of the primary tumor is a cornerstone of management in RMS. Thus, the approach to 
comprehensive care of these patients requires multidisciplinary discussion inclusive of oncologists, surgeons, and radiation oncologists.
• The basic principles of wide and complete resection of the primary tumor with a surrounding envelope of normal tissue should be followed at the initial 
and/or subsequent operations wherever possible and reasonable. Adequate margins of uninvolved tissue are required unless this involves sacrifice of 
normal tissue that either cannot be resected or would result in an unacceptable loss of function or is not technically feasible. 
• Surgical excision should not be performed unless an R0 resection is believed to be possible without significant loss of form or function. While 
subjective, this should be discussed on a case-by-case basis and both short- and long-term complications and quality-of-life measures should be 
weighed while incorporating the patient’s expectations, risk tolerance, and preferences. Generally unacceptable morbidity includes orbital exenteration, 
major excisions of the face, extremity amputation, rendering an extremity useless, major organ resection (bladder/prostate resection, pneumonectomy), 
pelvic exenteration, or resection requiring permanent stool/urinary diversion.
• The initial surgical procedure (a biopsy or excision as employed for benign tumor) may have been performed prior to establishing the diagnosis and 
may result in a situation in which there is: 1) gross residual tumor; 2) microscopically involved margins; or 3) uncertainty as to margins or residual tumor. 
Under these circumstances, the concept of PRE is advisable and should be applied wherever feasible, unless the resulting disability is considered 
unacceptable. This means wide re-excision of the previous operative site, including an adequate "envelope" of normal tissue, with careful marking and 
examination of all margins. Clinical Group assignment will be determined on the basis of pathology from the definitive operation prior to the start of 
multimodal therapy. 
Outcomes for patients with Group I status, including when PRE is performed, are superior to outcomes of patients with Group II or III status, even with 
DPE with consideration of maintaining form and function. 
• Debulking procedures do not improve outcomes and thus are not considered as part of surgical therapy.
• It is unclear what role excision of the biopsy tract has on outcome in RMS specifically. However, there are data to suggest that tumor cells can seed the 
biopsy tract and that this risk is higher in open biopsy tracts. Therefore, the biopsy tract should be excised if able. This consideration is greater in open 
tracts versus percutaneous approaches.
• All specimens should be oriented for pathology to determine margins. Narrow margins may be unavoidable in some sites such as in the head and 
neck. In these situations, the surgeon should take separate biopsies of the “normal” tissue around the margins of the resection cavity, and submit these 
separately for pathologic review.
• The surgical bed should be marked for potential future RT.
• In patients who did not undergo resection but have a residual mass following definitive chemotherapy and definitive RT, excision is not recommended.
Biopsy
• Tumors in the head, neck, orbit, parameningeal, or some genitourinary sites (ie, vulva, vagina) are frequently not amenable to primary excision 
(excisional biopsy). These tumors should undergo biopsy only (incisional or core needle).  
• Both open incisional biopsy and image-guided core needle (tru-cut) biopsies are appropriate.
• Endoscopic biopsies are appropriate for bladder, prostate, or vaginal tumors.
• Biopsy should provide enough tissue for histologic subtyping, fusion status, IHC, NGS, fusion testing, and, where possible, tissue for biobanking. See
Principles of Pathologic Assessment.
PRMS-D
1 OF 2

Site Specific
• These sites are rarely amenable to upfront resection and biopsy is generally recommended: orbital, parameningeal, vulvar/vaginal, biliary, or bladder/
prostate tumors. These tumors can rarely be excised without notable loss of form or function. Thus, upfront biopsy for diagnosis followed by systemic 
therapy is the initial approach.
• Paratesticular:
Resection should only occur by radical orchiectomy via inguinal approach. If prior scrotal resection with negative margins, hemiscrotectomy is no 
longer routinely recommended.
Hemiscrotectomy should only be performed when there is invasion of the scrotal skin.
 ◊Reflexive completion hemiscrotectomy is no longer recommended for patients who had a transscrotal biopsy or previous partial scrotal resection if 
there is no evidence of any further involvement of the scrotal skin. 
• Vulva, Vagina, Uterus Fundus/Cervix:
Biopsy, staging, and chemotherapy should be the initial approach—not radical excision. Vulvar/vaginal sites can often be managed with chemotherapy 
alone or chemotherapy with RT (EBRT or brachytherapy) and typically do not need surgical intervention beyond biopsy. High-dose alkylating agents 
are options in select patients with a proven complete response from repeat biopsies.1
Some uterine tumors may be appropriately managed with upfront surgery (hysterectomy), allowing for Stage 1 Group 1 assignment, with potential for 
reduced adjuvant chemotherapy and omission of RT. 
 ◊Initial radical hysterectomy may be considered for patients who do not desire future fertility after shared decision-making with the treating team. For 
patients who desire future fertility, recommend biopsy followed by local control with attempts at preserving fertility.1
Recommend including a pediatric adolescent gynecologist or reproductive infertility endocrinologist within the multidisciplinary team when discussing 
local control treatments.
• Biliary: 
This site rarely needs radical surgery, as disease typically responds to multimodal therapy; thus, surgery is reserved for diagnosis and staging. This 
includes for patients with biliary obstruction, which also typically improves with chemotherapy.
• Extremity:
Only the involved compartment should be traversed for biopsy.
Incisions should be made along the axis of the involved compartment.
• Bladder/Prostate: 
Biopsy, staging, and chemotherapy should be the initial approach rather than upfront cystectomy/prostatectomy.  
In rare circumstances when the tumor only involves the dome of the bladder, partial cystectomy can be considered as long as bladder function is 
maintained.

PRMS-E
1 OF 2
Lymph Node Disease Assessment at Diagnosis
• Clinical and/or imaging evaluation of regional lymph nodes should be performed pretreatment and preoperatively and is an important part of 
pretreatment staging. 
• Clinically or radiographically involved (>1 cm in short axis on CT/MRI, or FDG avid) nodes should be sampled for histologic evaluation.
In those patients with pathologically confirmed lymph nodes or clinically/radiographically involved nodes that are not assessed pathologically, 
treatment with RT to the regional nodal bed is required (PRMS-F).
• Positive lymph nodes outside of the regional basin should be classified as distant spread (Clinical Group IV, Stage 4).
• Nodal evaluation by open biopsy is recommended, preferably using sentinel node technique; however, core needle biopsy may be appropriate. If 
sentinel node evaluation is not available, then random regional lymph node sampling is the next most appropriate method of surgical evaluation for 
most sites.
• In general, prophylactic radical node dissection, as employed for some other malignancies, is not necessary in childhood RMS. 
• Nodal sampling is strongly recommended for patients with known FOXO1 gene fusion-positive disease or unknown FOXO1 status and alveolar 
histology.
• Extremity tumors:
Systematic regional lymph node sampling is required even when there are no palpable involved nodes. Excisional biopsy, when feasible, is 
recommended.
 ◊If the nodes are clinically involved, biopsy of more proximal nodes should be performed before proceeding with sampling.
Tumors arising in the shoulder and hip girdle are considered to be extremity tumors.
• Paratesticular tumors:
RPLN sampling is required for all patients ≥10 years of age with paratesticular tumors and patients <10 years with clinically or radiographically 
involved lymph nodes (except when extensive lymph node involvement is identified by imaging studies). If there is extensive gross node involvement, 
then only confirmatory node biopsy is recommended and the patient's disease is classified as Clinical Group III.
Patients with positive nodes on CT scan should have staging ipsilateral template nerve-sparing RPLN dissection (RPLND) or ipsilateral infra-renal 
lymph node sampling of 7–12 nodes.1 If all nodal disease is removed, the patient's disease is classified as Clinical Group II B or C.
Any positive node(s) on CT scan not completely removed places the patient's disease in Clinical Group III.
• Head and Neck: 
The incidence of cervical node involvement varies in head and neck primaries.2 Cervical lymph node biopsy is not required unless nodes are clinically 
involved. If so, a biopsy should be performed.

Regional Nodal Basins for RMS2
• Extremity:
Lower Extremity – inguinal, femoral, popliteal nodes (rarely involved) 
Upper Extremity – axillary, brachial, epitrochlear, infraclavicular nodes 
• Genitourinary: 
Bladder/Prostate – pelvic, retroperitoneal nodes at renal artery level or below 
Cervix and Uterus Fundus – pelvic, retroperitoneal nodes at renal artery level or below 
Paratesticular – pelvic, retroperitoneal nodes at renal artery level or below 
Vagina – retroperitoneal, pelvic nodes at or below common iliacs nodes, inguinal nodes
Vulva – inguinal nodes 
• Head and Neck: 
Head/Neck – ipsilateral cervical, jugular, preauricular, occipital, supraclavicular nodes for laterally placed tumors (excluding scalp); may have bilateral 
adenopathy with centrally placed tumors 
Orbit/Eyelid – ipsilateral jugular, preauricular, cervical nodes 
• Intrathoracic:
internal mammary, mediastinal nodes 
• Retroperitoneum/Pelvis:
pelvic, retroperitoneal nodes 
• Trunk: 
Abdominal Wall – inguinal, femoral nodes 
Chest Wall – axillary, internal mammary, infraclavicular nodes 
• Other: 
Biliary/Liver – liver hilar nodes 
Perianal/Perineal – inguinal, pelvic nodes; may be bilateral

header: PRINCIPLES OF RADIATION THERAPY
content:
PRMS-F
1 OF 3
General:
• Treatment with photons, electrons, or protons may all be appropriate, depending on the clinical circumstances. 
• Advanced RT technologies may be used to spare important organs at risk (OARs) and decrease the risk for late normal tissue damage while still 
achieving the primary goal of local tumor control. Advanced technologies including intensity-modulated RT (IMRT)/volumetric modulated arc therapy 
(VMAT), image-guided RT (IGRT), or proton beam therapy may offer significant and clinically relevant advantages.
• Dose-sparing for OARs reflects best clinical practice, as it reduces the risk of complications from normal tissue damage. Achieving highly conformal 
dose distributions is especially important for patients who are being treated with curative intent or who have long life expectancies following therapy.1
• Since the advantages of these techniques include tightly conformal doses and steep gradients next to normal tissues, careful target delineation and 
treatment delivery verification are critical to avoid the risk of tumor geographic miss and subsequent decrease in tumor control. 
• Initial and post-induction diagnostic imaging with contrast-enhanced CT, MRI, FDG-PET/CT or FDG-PET/MRI, US, and other imaging modalities 
facilitate target definition. 
• Image guidance with photon and proton beam therapy is typically required to provide assurance of accurate daily delivery.
Timing:
• Typically, primary site RT starts at week 13. 
• Patients who require emergency RT (eg, spinal cord compression, vision loss) may receive RT to their emergency site starting at week 1. The entire 
course of the emergency RT should be administered starting at week 1, rather than splitting the treatment and concluding the course at week 13.
• Patients who undergo DPE will still receive RT. DPE does not obviate the need for RT, but it may allow for a lower dose.
•  Dactinomycin is held during RT.
• Typically, metastatic site RT is given after completion of standard chemotherapy. However, in some cases, metastatic sites may be irradiated 
concurrently with the primary site.
Planning Considerations:
• Patients should be simulated with appropriate immobilization.
• Four-dimensional (4D)-CT is recommended for targets that are subject to motion. 
• Relevant CT and/or MRI studies performed at baseline and after induction chemotherapy should be fused to the CT simulation for target delineation. 
• Primary site target volume definitions:
The initial gross tumor volume (GTV1) will include the prechemotherapy extent of disease as visualized on baseline diagnostic imaging, respecting 
pushing borders.
The boost GTV (GTV2) will include the post-induction chemotherapy extent of disease as visualized on pre-RT imaging.
The clinical target volumes (CTV1, CTV2) = GTV1 + 1 cm and GTV2 + 1 cm, respectively, confined to anatomical borders. For instance, the CTV 
should not extend into uninvolved lungs, kidneys, abdominal organs, or bones. 
If the tumor is subject to motion, an internal target volume (ITV) should be utilized based on the 4D-CT. 
The planning target volume (PTV) = CTV + 0.3–0.5 cm, depending on the institutional standard, immobilization method, and modality of in-room 
verification imaging.

Nodal Treatment:
• Pathologically involved nodal basins should be irradiated. Uninvolved nodal basins should not be irradiated electively.
Radiographically suspicious (ie, enlarged) lymph nodes may be presumed to be involved and irradiated if biopsy is not feasible. 
Consider irradiating FDG-avid lymph nodes if biopsy is not feasible.
Metastatic Site RT:2-4
• RT is recommended for metastatic sites, as feasible. Various doses and fractionation schedules (conventional, hypofractionation, stereotactic body RT 
[SBRT]) are acceptable, depending upon the site and clinical scenario.
• Whole lung irradiation (WLI) is recommended in patients with upfront pulmonary metastases. The WLI dose is 15 Gy in 10 fractions. Residual 
parenchymal lung metastases at the time of RT should be considered for either resection or a radiotherapy boost.

Site-Specific Considerations:

d Higher dose of cyclophosphamide is associated with prolonged neutropenia resulting in increased risk of infection and sepsis. Patients with high-risk RMS and Oberlin 
risk factors (ORF) >2 have poorer prognosis.10,11 Consider low- or intermediate-dose cumulative cyclophosphamide regimens (8.4–16.8 mg/m2) in those patients 
with poor performance score and high ORF. Consider outpatient regimens utilizing oral mesna and outpatient IV fluids in coordination with goals of care. Concordant 
chemotherapy and/or RT may offer symptom relief.

header:
PRINCIPLES OF SURVIVORSHIP
RHABDOMYOSARCOMA
content:
•  As the cure rate for soft tissue sarcomas continues to increase to nearly 75%,1 there is growing focus on the long-term side effects that affect patients 
following the completion of therapy. Oncology Guidelines defines cancer survivorship as starting “from the time of diagnosis, through the balance of life” (https://www.
Oncology Guidelines/professionals/physician_gls/pdf/survivorship.pdf; adapted with permission from the National Coalition for Cancer Survivorship as shown in 
the National Cancer Institute’s Office of Cancer Survivorship Definitions web page, available at https://cancercontrol.cancer.gov/ocs/definitions). The 
Children’s Oncology Group (COG) has a similar definition and emphasis on survivorship (https://www.survivorshipguidelines.org).
• The National Academy of Medicine has recommended that all cancer survivors be provided with a survivorship care plan (SCP) once they complete 
treatment. These plans should be specific to the patient and based on the patient’s cancer, their treatment, the age of the patient at the time of 
treatment, and the patient’s comorbidities.
• While there is growing recognition of the importance of survivorship, despite long-standing recommendations from numerous bodies, most survivors of 
childhood cancer are not receiving even basic screenings for serious late effects.2
• Being knowledgeable about the potential late effects of treatment, discussing late effects with patients and caregivers, and creating a SCP for 
monitoring and screening is within the purview of the treating oncology team. 
• It is outside the scope of the Oncology Guidelines for Pediatric Soft Tissue Sarcoma to comprehensively discuss all the potential late effects that a patient 
with a soft tissue sarcoma might experience. It is also not  possible to use this document to create a tailored SCP. Nevertheless, we have provided 
some general information about late effects based on potential exposure, as well as quality resources that focus on survivorship and late effects—some 
of which can be used to create an SCP (https://www.survivorshipguidelines.org/pdf/2023/COG_LTFU_Guidelines_Comprehensive_v6.pdf; https://www.
passportforcare.org/en). Additionally, there are risk prediction calculators through the Childhood Cancer Survivorship Study for the following conditions: 
breast cancer, cardiomyopathy (derived using SJLIFE data), cardiovascular risks, and kidney failure (https://ccss.stjude.org/resources/calculators.html).
Oncofertility and Fertility Preservation:
• Many regimens for the treatment for pediatric soft tissue sarcomas are partially or very gonadotoxic.3 Evidence has demonstrated that survivors place a 
great importance on being able to conceive and have biologically related children,4,5 and yet, many to most are not provided with adequate information 
regarding infertility risk and access to fertility preservation resources.6,7 There are resources to adequately assess a patient’s risk for infertility, and what 
fertility preservation options should be offered.3,8-10 Additionally, there are risk prediction calculators through the Childhood Cancer Survivorship Study 
for Acute Ovarian Failure Risk and Primary Ovarian Insufficiency (https://ccss.stjude.org/resources/calculators.html).

header:

RHABDOMYOSARCOMA
content:
Consequential Risks of Treatment: 
• Please note that there are many additional and consequential risks to pediatric cancer survivors irrespective of cancer type and therapy given, such as 
psychosocial, neurocognitive, financial hardship, etc. 
Please see COG Long-Term Follow-Up for further guidance and recommendations.
• Chemotherapy:
Vincristine: peripheral sensory or motor neuropathy, vasospastic attacks (Raynaud’s phenomenon).
Dactinomycin: no known late effects.
Cyclophosphamide: ovarian or testicular hormonal dysfunction, diminished ovarian reserve, impaired spermatogenesis, infertility, secondary leukemia, 
myelodysplasia, urinary tract toxicity, bladder malignancy.
Irinotecan: no known late effects.
Vinorelbine: no known late effects.
• Radiation:
All fields: subsequent benign or malignant neoplasm, dermatologic toxicity.
Brain/cranium: brain tumor (benign or malignant), neurocognitive deficits, clinical leukoencephalopathy, cerebrovascular complications, craniofacial 
abnormalities, chronic sinusitis.
Neuroendocrine axis: overweight/obesity, growth hormone deficiency, precocious puberty, hyperprolactinemia, central hypothyroidism, gonadotropin 
deficiency, central adrenal insufficiency.
Eye: cataracts, ocular toxicity, ototoxicity.
Oral cavity: xerostomia; salivary gland dysfunction, dental abnormalities, temporomandibular joint dysfunction, osteonecrosis of the jaw.
Neck/thyroid: thyroid nodules, thyroid cancer, hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, carotid artery disease, subclavian artery disease.
Breast: breast cancer, breast tissue hypoplasia.
Lungs: pulmonary toxicity, lung cancer.
Heart: cardiac toxicity.
Spleen: functional asplenia.
GI/hepatic system: esophageal stricture, impaired glucose metabolism/diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, hepatic toxicity, cholelithiasis, bowel 
obstruction, chronic enterocolitis, fistula, strictures, colorectal cancer.
Urinary tract: renal toxicity, urinary tract toxicity, bladder malignancy.
Male reproductive system: testicular hormonal dysfunction, impaired spermatogenesis.
Female reproductive system: ovarian hormone deficiencies, diminished ovarian reserve, uterine vascular insufficiency, vaginal fibrosis/stenosis. 
Musculoskeletal system: musculoskeletal growth problems, scoliosis/kyphosis, radiation-induced fracture.

Consequential Risks of Treatment (continued):
• Surgery: 
Amputation: amputation-related complications.
Central venous catheter: thrombosis, vascular insufficiency, infection of retained cuff or line tract, post-thrombotic syndrome.
Cystectomy: cystectomy-related complications.
Enucleation: impaired cosmesis, poor prosthetic fit, orbital hypoplasia.
Hysterectomy: pelvic floor dysfunction, urinary incontinence, sexual dysfunction.
Laparotomy: adhesions, bowel obstruction.
Limb sparing procedure: complications related to limb-sparing procedure.
Nephrectomy: hydrocele (male), renal toxicity.
Neurosurgery-brain: neurocognitive deficits, motor and/or sensory deficits, seizures, hydrocephalus, shunt malformation, overweight/obesity, diabetes 
insipidus.
Neurosurgery-spinal cord: neurogenic bladder, urinary incontinence, neurogenic bowel, fecal incontinence, psychosexual dysfunction, scoliosis/
kyphosis.
Oophoropexy: oophoropexy-related complications.
Oophorectomy: ovarian hormone deficiencies, diminished ovarian reserve, loss of ovarian follicular pool.
Orchiectomy: testicular hormonal dysfunction, impaired spermatogenesis.
Pelvic surgery/cystectomy: urinary incontinence, urinary tract obstruction, fecal incontinence, psychosexual dysfunction, sexual dysfunction, infertility.
Splenectomy: asplenia.
Thoracic surgery: pulmonary dysfunction, scoliosis/kyphosis.
Thyroidectomy: hypothyroidism.
Genetic Predisposition Syndromes:
• Many studies of individual genetic disease cohorts demonstrate that children with certain genetic syndromes develop RMS more frequently than those 
unaffected.11 Cancer predisposition syndromes associated with RMS include Li-Fraumeni,12 neurofibromatosis type 1,13 DICER1-related,14 Costello,15 
Noonan,16 and other RASopathies, including mosaic RASopathies.17 
• A recent study that examined a large, unselected cohort of pediatric patients with RMS found that 7.3% carried a cancer predisposition variant.18 The 
chance of an individual patient having cancer predisposition syndrome is affected by the histologic type of RMS,18 presence of anaplasia,19 FOXO1 
fusion status,18 age,18,19 location,20 and tumor somatic mutations.18 
• Therefore, besides the family history of a patient, individual characteristics of the presentation and the tumor should be strongly considered, as germline 
testing may be warranted.

Tumor Size (S):
• a: ≤5 cm in diameter
• b: >5 cm in diameter
Regional Nodes (N):
• N0: Regional nodes not clinically involved
• N1: Regional nodes clinically involved as defined as ≥1 cm measured in 
short axis on CT or MRI
• Nx: Clinical status of regional nodes unknown (especially sites that preclude 
lymph node evaluation)
Metastases (M):
• M0: No distant metastases
• M1: Distant metastases present
Note: The presence of positive cytology in pleural fluid, abdominal fluid, or 
CSF and the presence of implants on pleural or peritoneal are considered 
evidence of metastasis.
Clinical Group
Definition
Group I
• Localized disease, completely resected
Note: Gross inspection and microscopic confirmation of complete resection is required.
Note: Regional nodes cannot be involved.
Group II
• Localized disease, grossly resected with microscopic residual disease, or
• Regional disease, grossly resected with or without microscopic residual disease
a. Localized disease, grossly resected tumor with microscopic residual disease, regional nodes not involved.
b. Regional disease with involved nodes, completely resected with no microscopic residual (including most distal node is histologically negative).
c. Regional disease with involved nodes, grossly resected with evidence of microscopic residual and/or histologic involvement of the most distal 
regional node in the dissection.
Group III
• Localized or regional disease, biopsy only or incomplete resection with gross residual disease
Group IV
• Distant metastatic disease present at onset
• Although not limited to these, the following are considered evidence of metastatic disease: 
1) The presence of positive cytology in CSF
2) Positive cytology in pleural or abdominal fluids, or 
3) The presence of implants on pleural or peritoneal surfaces.
Note: Regional lymph node involvement and adjacent organ infiltration are not considered metastatic disease.
Note: The presence of a pleural effusion or ascites without positive cytologic evaluation is not considered evidence of metastatic disease.

CAT-1
Oncology Guidelines Categories of Evidence and Consensus
Category 1
Based upon high-level evidence (≥1 randomized phase 3 trials or high-quality, robust meta-analyses), there is 
uniform Oncology Guidelines consensus (≥85% support of the Panel) that the intervention is appropriate.
Category 2A
Based upon lower-level evidence, there is uniform Oncology Guidelines consensus (≥85% support of the Panel) that the 
intervention is appropriate.
Category 2B
Based upon lower-level evidence, there is Oncology Guidelines consensus (≥50%, but <85% support of the Panel) that the 
intervention is appropriate.
Category 3
Based upon any level of evidence, there is major Oncology Guidelines disagreement that the intervention is appropriate. 
All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Categories of Preference
Preferred
Interventions that are based on superior efficacy, safety, and evidence; and, when appropriate, 
affordability.
Other recommended
Other interventions that may be somewhat less efficacious, more toxic, or based on less mature data; 
or significantly less affordable for similar outcomes.
Useful in certain 
circumstances
Other interventions that may be used for selected patient populations (defined with recommendation).
All recommendations are considered appropriate.
